reader_relationship: fellow enthusiast sharing a personal workaround with a peer, not an authority
word_choice_style: Very casual, with slang (Af), missing apostrophes in contractions (dont, isnt, its, thats, doesnt), occasional lowercase 'i', and everyday vocabulary. Uses contractions but without apostrophes. Direct and simple. Prefers blunt confirmations/negations: nope, nah, yep. Avoids formal business jargon; any technical terms are either explained briefly or assumed to be known.
sentence_construction: Short to medium sentences, often simple or run-on. Clauses joined loosely with commas or no punctuation. Fragments used. Loose, stream-of-consciousness syntax.
punctuation_details: Apostrophes consistently omitted in contractions. Hyphens used for compounds (markdown-based, note-taking). No em dash used. Bullet points lack terminal punctuation if items are short or fragments. No semicolons. Periods end most sentences but are sometimes omitted after single-word closing (Cya). No ellipsis marks used.
rhythm_description: Choppy, staccato rhythm overall. The writer begins sections with a longer statement, then shifts into rapid-fire bullet points. Bullet lists dominate. Varies between one-liners and very short fragments. Avoid three parallel items in a row in prose; if listing three items, use a bullet list or vary structure.
sentence_opener_patterns: I, You, It, Because, Yeah, So, TL;DR. Also occasional 'Nope', 'Yeah i get it'. Bullet points open with dashes and subject fragments (e.g., '- Its not a matter of...', '- You can...').
sentence_ending_patterns: Ends with period typically. Bullet points without periods if they are fragments. Headings end with question marks or nothing. The article ends with the exact heading 'Thats it Cya.' without any punctuation after the phrase. No trailing period.
hedging_style: Minimal. Occasional 'maybe for you too'. Generally assertive or offhand, not tentative.
emphasis_style: All-caps slang (Af) after an adjective, capitalization of key terms (Title), and blunt phrasing (flawless, perfect, nope, nah, yep). No italics or underline for emphasis; bold sparingly used for headings.
analogy_style: Rare. Uses only very short analogies or pop-culture references (e.g., Jack Harlow). Avoids extended analogies or hypothetical scenarios that span multiple sentences. If used, keeps to a single sentence or less.
humor_style: Understated and self-deprecating (slow Af), playful framing of headings (huh what?, Cya). Light tone, not overtly jokey.
argument_style: Avoids formal argument structures. When the skeleton requires counterargument or objection handling, the writer does not set up an attributed opposition (e.g., 'Some say X...'). Instead, presents the point directly as a personal observation or assertion (e.g., 'I hear that but...' as a natural aside). Uses bullet points and personal testimony to persuade, not a claim-evidence-warrant chain. Never uses extended third-person case studies; all examples are first-person and brief (no more than 2-3 sentences).
jargon_style: Introduces domain terms (e.g., Markdown, graph view, Syncthing, PMF, MVP) without elaborate explanation or defines them briefly in parentheses. Assumes reader knows the domain or can infer. Does not avoid jargon but never uses overly technical language.
parenthetical_style: Infrequent parentheticals; when they appear they are short asides (e.g., in my POV, like jack harlow says…). Not a habitual feature. Never uses em dashes for parentheticals.
structural_conventions: Short sections with markdown headers (###, ##). Heavy use of bullet lists. A TL;DR section with a colon directly after 'TL;DR' must appear before the closing 'Thats it Cya.' heading. The closing heading is 'Thats it Cya.' exactly, as a markdown heading (## Thats it Cya.) with no additional punctuation. Section headers derived from the content skeleton must be rewritten into the characteristic conversational, fragmentary style that includes a quirk such as 'huh what?' or '(in my POV)' (e.g., 'Second Brain huh what?', 'Why validate? (in my POV)'). Every section heading must have one of these markers. Numbered steps used only if mandated by skeleton, written as plain numbers (1., 2.) or as bullet dashes. When the skeleton includes enumerations (e.g., three common fixes that fail), render them as a bullet list, not as parallel sentences.

voice_fingerprints.pattern: Consistently omits apostrophes in all contractions: writes dont, isnt, its, thats, cya, doesnt instead of don't, isn't, it's, that's, see you, doesn't.

Every section heading is rewritten into a conversational, fragmentary phrase that includes a signature quirk marker such as 'huh what?' or '(in my POV)'. This applies even when the skeleton provides a formal heading; the heading must be transformed to match this style.
Uses 'Af' (capitalized) immediately after an adjective for emphasis (e.g., slow Af).
Inconsistent capitalization: occasionally writes 'i' lowercase in the middle of sentences, and other lowercase instances where standard English would capitalize (e.g., 'Yeah i get it').
Writes a TL;DR section with a colon directly after 'TL;DR', and ends the article with the exact markdown heading 'Thats it Cya.' (with hash marks) without any additional punctuation.
Uses bullet lists where items are a mix of full sentences and fragments, without terminal punctuation if the item is a fragment.

Presents arguments through direct personal statements and first-person anecdotes, never through attributed objections or extended third-person case studies. Any objection is stated as a personal observation (e.g., 'I think that misses the point').

negative_space: Never uses perfectly correct grammar: apostrophes always omitted, occasional subject-verb disagreement, run-on sentences without proper punctuation.
Never uses formal argument structures: no attributed objections/rebuttals (e.g., 'Funded founders say...'), no elaborately named sections like 'Section 1: The Hook'.
Never uses extended third-person case study narratives that span multiple paragraphs. All examples must be first-person and no longer than 2-3 sentences.
Never uses em dashes with spaces or as standard punctuation; avoids elaborate parenthetical asides.
Never writes in a detached third-person narrative; always uses first or second person, strongly personal (I, you).
Never uses multi-sentence extended analogies or hypothetical scenarios to illustrate a point.
Never uses inclusive 'we' or 'let's'; always 'I' and 'you' individually.
Never uses negative parallel constructions such as 'It's not X, it's Y' or 'It wasn't X, it was Y'; states assertions directly without inversion.
Never uses ellipsis (...) for dramatic pause or trailing off.
Never overuses rule-of-three patterns: avoids three sequential parallel structures in prose. If listing three items, use a bullet list.

Never omits the TL;DR section before the closing 'Thats it Cya.' heading, and never alters the closing phrase or adds punctuation to it. The closing must be a markdown heading, not bolded or plain text.
Never uses promotional puffery language that makes vague positive assertions without specific evidence (e.g., 'perfect product', 'strong traction' without concrete support).
Never uses placeholder links or meta-instructions like '[Placeholder Link]' without integrating them naturally into the narrative.
open_observations: The writing feels like a spoken monologue transcribed. Stream-of-consciousness, unpolished, with rough edges that become a trademark. The author is comfortable with imperfection, which makes the voice feel authentic and approachable. The writer's voice is consistent across topics, but adaptability to structured content skeletons requires care to preserve the casual style. Most importantly, the heading style with conversational markers (huh what?, in my POV) is a non-negotiable signature that must appear on every section heading. The writer never deviates from this. Additionally, the writer never uses AI-typical patterns such as three-item rhetorical series, negative inversions, or attributed objection rebuttals. These must be actively avoided.
